extracted.brandBackground: Code Ninjas operates child-focused learning centers providing educational programs in computer programming, coding, math, logic, and teamwork.

hiddenCosts.name: Pre-Opening Minimum Royalty Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Fee of $500 per month if the center is not open by the agreed opening deadline.

hiddenCosts.name: Convention Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Annual fee of $750 per attendee if a franchise convention is held.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: The franchise agreement requires you to resolve disputes by mediation and litigation in Georgia.

operationalRisks.title: Financial Condition
operationalRisks.description: The franchisor's financial condition calls into question its ability to provide services and support.

operationalRisks.title: Mandatory Minimum Payments
operationalRisks.description: You must make minimum royalty or advertising fund payments if you have not opened after the deadline.

operationalRisks.title: Turnover Rate
operationalRisks.description: During the last 3 years, 92 outlets were terminated, not renewed, reacquired, or ceased operations.

extracted.conceptRationale: Code Ninjas provides child-focused STEM and computer programming educational classes.
extracted.staffingRationale: Requires a full-time Center Director and trained instructional staff to run daily operations.

riskReasons: A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.
notes: Average rent not found; fixed costs may be understated.

underwriting.assessment: $250,000 liquid covers the $220,000 mid-point build-out without financing, though that leaves little working-capital cushion. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $16,227/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: Code Ninjas provides child-focused STEM and computer programming educational classes.

benchmark.label: Education / childcare
benchmark.dominantRisk: Regulated staff ratios and enrollment/utilization. Labor dominates and ratios cap how lean you can run.
considerations: Labor is the dominant line at ~40–55% and is floored by regulated student/staff ratios.
Validate enrollment ramp and capacity-utilization curves with Item 20 operators.
Confirm licensing, facility, and compliance costs specific to your state — not in the FDD.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 10–20% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Occupancy / rent (not disclosed)
buildup.note: Item 7 disclosed no rent figure — benchmark occupancy applied so the line above isn't overstated

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 2.3–3.1 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Education / childcare” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Item 7 disclosed no rent — occupancy estimated at the 10–18% category band.
Estimated at the 5–12% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 40–55% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor flagged its own financial condition as a risk. The figures we extracted look comparatively stable, so confirm directly against the most recent statements.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut. There is no parent guarantee of performance in this FDD, so Code Ninjas Holdings LLC is not contractually obligated to keep funding the franchisor or backing its commitments to you.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: no parent guarantee backing the franchisor's obligations to you
elevated unit closures (13 in the last year, about 5% of the system)
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
